title: [Feature]: Do "podman machine init" and "podman machine start" after installation on Windows
body:
### Feature request description

I'm trying to make it as easy as possible for our users to install Podman on Windows for use with VS Code Dev Containers. Currently, they have to:

* Run the podman installer.
* Open a terminal, and then run "podman machine init" and "podman machine start".

### Suggest potential solution

Add an option to the installer to do the two machine commands as part of the installer to get a ready-to-use environment after the installer completes.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

Having a wrapper script around the installer is tricky due to the multi-stage installation when WSL2 is installed as well. The installer continues after the reboot, but the script which would do the podman machine init/start commands won't. There are, of course, ways around that, but it feels common enough to be an option in the installer.

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body: @imphil Podman desktop aims to give you this experience, along with auto starting your machine instances for you. Could you try that out and let us know what you think?

I have given Podman Desktop a try and I feel it gives me a worse installation experience than only using Podman. Let me explain my thinking, and please jump in where I misunderstood or misconfigured things!

To start, this is the **user story** I'm trying to support: *As a Windows user, I want to contribute to a project which provides a pre-configured [VS Code Dev Container](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/devcontainers/containers). As user, I don't care about the fact that I'm running a WSL2 VM or a container. I want to use podman instead of Docker Desktop (the default option) mostly for licensing reasons.*

VS Code Dev Containers make it transparent to the user that they are running a container; it all happens in the background. However, users need to get to the point where they have a working container runtime, i.e., they need to install podman and all associated dependencies.

I have been exploring two options: Podman Desktop and the Podman installer.

When using **Podman Desktop** on a freshly installed Windows 10 machine I experienced this flow:
* Download Podman Desktop.
* Install it. The installer is completely silent, which is nice.
* Run Podman Desktop.
* Find the Dashboard, and realize that podman itself needs to be installed.
* Do the requirements check. First Hyper-V wasn't enabled. Follow the link to the Microsoft documentation on how to enable it, and restart (I think? Not sure any more.) Start Podman Desktop again manually after the reboot.
* Redo the requirements check, now WSL2 is missing. Follow the link to a Microsoft documentation page how to install WSL2 manually. (The link was actually misleading, I fixed that in https://github.com/containers/podman-desktop/pull/1274.)
* Redo the requirements check again, now podman is missing. Download the podman installer, and install it.
* (My memory is blurry after this point, but now the machine needs to be created and started.)

When using the **Podman installer**, the steps boil down to:

* Download the Podman installer, run it.
* Keep the "WSL2" checkbox checked, which automatically installs WSL2 and enables Hyper-V.
* Open a terminal, type "podman machine init; podman machine start".
* Done.

**Observations:**
* Podman Desktop does too much checking for things which don't need to be checked for, but could be (and mostly are already in the Podman installer) automated.
* Ideally, podman would already be up and running after the installer of Podman Desktop completes.
* Podman Desktop provides a fair amount of options in its UI. My user doesn't care about any of that -- they exclusively build, run, and interact with the container through VS Code.

@imphil  thanks for such a detailed overview! Are you looking at contributing this approach as a patch to vscode (custom extension or patch direct to devcontainers)? Or do you mean you are looking at more of an appliance based use-case, in that every time the user gets a fresh install? Relatedly do you also expect podman machine to automatically restart after every reboot?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Sorry for the delay. We have been experimenting a fair bit on a various ways to improve the user experience and I started a write-up on it, but didn't find time to finish it yet. I'll get back in the coming days.

Similarly, I think, to @imphil, I am developing an app which I don't want users to have to know that containers/Podman/WSL2 is running. If users don't have WSL2 installed, there will be a required reboot on `podman machine init`. Also, there are a couple of window prompts, I would like to be able to hid/silence the first window popup (perhaps with a `--quiet` flag for `init` command?). The second window popup reboots Windows on confirmation, so that is necessary. There is also a Powershell pop-up on start-up that could be hidden if `--quiet` is used with init? Currently, I or users do not need to run machine start after startup IF `--now` is used with init which is nice. PS: Just want to say how awesome it has been to find Podman and use it in my project. Having an open-source, well-documented project for all of this has been a life-saver. Much appreciation to the Podman devs & team!

Additional observation:
- podman-desktop (admittedly a different project) provides an airgapped installer (love it); however, it doesn't really provide any benefit since 1) podman-desktop does nothing without the engine, and 2) `podman machine init` requires internet access.
@mwtrigg in airgap mode podman desktop provides a local binary for the 'podman machine init' command so it doesn't require internet connection

> [@mwtrigg](https://github.com/mwtrigg) in airgap mode podman desktop provides a local binary for the 'podman machine init' command so it doesn't require internet connection

I have not found this to be an accurate statement. I'm using airgapped podman desktop v1.15.0. After installation of it and then running "podman machine init", I get:

> Error: pinging container registry quay.io: Get "http://quay.io/v2/" : dial tcp: lookup quay.io: no such host

Due to the complexity of the windows installer, there was a recent patch that removed the installation of the virtual machine provider.  As such, we cannot start anything now as recommended. 

We did add an error message that if podman commands are run, podman will now emit this:

```
Cannot connect to Podman. Please verify your connection to the Linux system using `podman system connection list`, or try `podman machine init` and `podman machine start` to manage a new Linux VM
```

I'm closing this feature because it is not currently possible.  I would be happy to re-open or contemplate a suggestion if folks have another one.
